backbone: a single {subject}, one isolated stylized game item floating in empty space, anime cel shaded hand-painted style, smooth simplified toony shapes with a clean readable silhouette, soft painted shading with two or three tonal steps per surface and gentle painted highlights, evenly lit from all sides so it casts no shadow anywhere, matte with no gloss, nothing glowing, no white outline, no pale rim stroke around the silhouette, no drop shadow, no cast shadow, no photographic detail, floating diagonally with the head at the upper left and the handle running down to the lower right, suspended with nothing beneath it, the whole item complete and uncropped as one connected piece, filling most of the frame, centered on a plain uniform flat light-grey backdrop, no ground, no surface, no horizon, no vignette, no text, no hands, no characters

prompts.label: classic cracked
prompts.subject: primitive stone hatchet shaped like a real camp hatchet, a rough cracked knapped dull matte grey stone wedge head with a deeply chipped jagged working edge in the same dull grey as the rest of the stone, capping the very top end of a warm brown wooden haft, lashed on with tan cord
prompts.extra: EVERY part of the stone head is the same dull matte medium-grey, including the chipped edge: no pale rim, no lighter band along the edge, no bright highlight, no sheen, no polish anywhere, because stone does not shine like iron. The head sits fully at the top end of the haft, edge facing sideways like an axe. A straight haft, neat cord lashing just under the head, mild painted wood grain.

prompts.label: chunky cracked

prompts.extra: EVERY part of the stone head is the same dull matte medium-grey, including the chipped edge: no pale rim, no lighter band along the edge, no bright highlight, no sheen, no polish anywhere, because stone does not shine like iron. Exaggerated proportions, the cracked head caps the top end of the haft, edge sideways. A stout haft with a flared pommel, broad cord lashing, mild painted wood grain.

notes: Round 2, unified set (owner decision 2026-07-19): ONE image per variant serves as BOTH the inventory icon and the image-to-3D mesh reference, because for items (unlike ore nodes, where the deposit and the yielded item genuinely differ) the icon and the model are the same object, and generating them separately made them look like different items. The single backbone keeps the ICON look the owner preferred (anime, toony, smooth, soft painted shading, less sculpted depth than round 1's mesh references) while carrying the mesh-lane requirements (single complete uncropped object, plain flat backdrop, rembg-friendly). The round-1 icons' whiteish rim stroke is banned (no white outline, no pale rim stroke); it would otherwise bake into the mesh texture as a halo. The drop shadow needed more than bans: with 'lying diagonally' Flux inferred a ground plane and painted an OPAQUE grey shadow no ban removed (its opaque core survives alpha-based stripping), so the backbone describes the item as FLOATING in empty space, evenly lit, suspended with nothing beneath it; 'as one connected piece' guards against the disassembled-tool failure a first batch produced. Framing is the inventory diagonal (head upper left, handle to lower right); the mesh build recovers upright via build_held.py's auto_level (an in-plane image rotation becomes a rotation about the Blender Y axis after import). Both pickaxes are pinned to the CLASSIC curved double-pointed miner's pick silhouette in every variant per owner feedback (round 1's stone pickaxe read as a hammer); the variant axis varies proportion and finish, never the head anatomy. Variant labels stay the round-1 four (faithful classic, chunky oversized, rough-hewn crude, sleek heroic) so the picker compares like with like.
